author: @charleskeller4288
text: Perhaps in the next video, you can explain the hold-down clamp placements in CAM, or is any clamp placement outside of the tool path fair game?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: anywhere outside the tool path mate

replies.author: @jackunger8634
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering Occasionally, even experienced CNC operators can manage to run a cutting bit or spindle or other moving part of the machine through a clamp. It's better to consider carefully than to gloss over this potential problem.
Good planning is also useful, to maintain or restore accurate part alignment during any needed clamp repositioning, for example for a part which requires both inside milling and outside milling.

author: @EricFranklin-d2v
text: Wow. That custom CAM software sure does look convenient👍

author: @TheCNCDen
text: to speed up internal holes, use an inside profile with an offset of a few thenths of a mm, then run a final pass at full depth as a clean up to dimension.

replies.author: @TheCNCDen
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Goes without saying add small tabs to original profile and clear the 'counter' before running final pass if you don't feel comfortable it being there.

author: @rogerwilson6367
text: When I first learned cnc  machining I would put something like a cocktail stick in the spindle (zero rpm) and set the Z high so that it didn't touch the workpiece. You can then watch the machining process without damaging the spinle/cutter etc. Keep the emergency stop close to hand also.

author: @EUPThatsMe
text: Even though g-code has a number of curved path types if you look at the file from a 3D slicer it is almost all straight line segments forming curves.  I've tried a few times to write g-code directly with very limited success and several broken end mills.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i must get around to looking at that sometime - cheers mate

author: @CrudelyMade
text:
I used inkscape to create vector (SVG) files and imported them into MakeraCAM, then added toolpaths. 
you can also import 3d STL files and then use makeraCAM to either create vector shapes or cut out the 3d shape. 
the shapes are easy, and you need to focus on which shape to create a toolpath for first, second, etc. 
if you just start with some basic shapes, squares and circles, it's pretty straight forward, allowing for fancier files pretty quickly. 
one thing to note: for 3d files makeraCAM doesn't support REST machining yet. 
(meaning a second operation doesn't realize an earlier operation has removed material and that the new tool doesn't need to remove that material again. )
this can make a secondary or tertiary operation take TONS of time instead of just cleaning up the part. 
otherwise it seems to work okay.  
NOTE: Lightburn makes GREAT (and affordable!) laser design and control software.. and they're working on a CNC version. 
Can't wait to see what they come up with.  If it keeps to their current standards, it should be pretty great.  something to keep an eye on. ;-)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: absolutely mate - thanks for the heads up
